topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The embossed nameplate on the classroom door hit me like a physical blow. Ms. Eleanor Vance. My former third-grade teacher. I stood frozen in the hallway, the polished linoleum suddenly feeling cold beneath my worn shoes. My son, Leo, was in Mrs. Vance’s class. The realization settled in my chest, a heavy, suffocating weight. I watched Leo, oblivious, laugh at something a classmate said, and a strange, hollow ache bloomed behind my ribs. I didn't move toward the classroom. Instead, I leaned against the cool cinderblock wall, my knuckles white where I gripped my purse strap. Every memory of that year—the forced enthusiasm, the way she’d praised his clumsy drawings—came rushing back, tainted now by this stark, unwelcome coincidence. I took a slow, ragged breath, the air tasting stale. I just needed to leave.

[story 2]

"No. Absolutely not." The words scraped out of my throat, sharp and brittle. I stared at the roster, my vision blurring slightly. Leo. My son. And Ms. Vance. The connection was undeniable, a grotesque thread tying my present unhappiness directly to that past experience. I felt a sudden, intense heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of pure resentment that made my jaw clench. I walked toward the classroom door, my steps deliberate, each one an act of forced control. When I reached the threshold, I didn't knock. I simply pushed the door open, the sound echoing unnervingly in the quiet room. I didn't look at Leo; my eyes were fixed on the empty desk where she used to stand. The air inside felt thick, charged with everything I wished I could discard.

[story 3]

I found myself hovering near the playground, watching Leo interact with a group of older children. Then, the familiar silhouette appeared—Ms. Vance, directing the game with that same overly bright, saccharine tone. A wave of profound dissatisfaction washed over me, making my stomach turn. It wasn't just recognition; it was a sickening confirmation of a pattern, a feeling that some things were irrevocably set in stone. I pulled my jacket tighter around myself, hunching my shoulders as if trying to shrink into the background. I watched them play, the scene playing out in slow motion, and a deep, simmering annoyance began to bubble up, hot and unpleasant. I shifted my weight, a restless energy vibrating just beneath my skin, a silent promise that I wouldn't be participating in this shared history again.
